description: Sourced aggregate targets v1 (verification pass 2026-07-13). Every VALUE here was read out of the primary source's own text/tables and is quoted in `statistic_location`; nothing is recalled or inferred. These are published AGGREGATE statistics only (no individual-level data). They drive aggregate-fit DIAGNOSTICS: the populations, definitions and time windows differ from this simulator's synthetic world (see per-target applicability_limits), so a small target distance is NOT validation, calibration, realism, or a prediction of any real platform. Targets whose numbers could NOT be verified against a source were removed to socio_sim/data/benchmarks/legacy_unsupported_*.json and are excluded from default output.

degree_tail_exponent.source: Barabasi & Albert 1999, 'Emergence of Scaling in Random Networks', Science 286:509 — actor-collaboration graph power-law exponent
degree_tail_exponent.source_title: Emergence of Scaling in Random Networks
degree_tail_exponent.population: movie-actor collaboration graph, N=212,250 vertices, mean degree 28.78 (the paper's only social network)
degree_tail_exponent.geography: not stated (Internet Movie Database actor corpus)
degree_tail_exponent.time_window: not stated (data as supplied to the authors, pre-1999)
degree_tail_exponent.methodology: power-law fit to the degree-distribution tail; the paper states the exponent with its own uncertainty
degree_tail_exponent.statistic_location: p.3 and Fig.1 caption: 'P(k) ~ k^-gamma_actor, where gamma_actor = 2.3 +/- 0.1'
degree_tail_exponent.tolerance_rationale: derived_from_source: the paper's own stated uncertainty on gamma_actor (+/- 0.1)
degree_tail_exponent.applicability_limits: An actor-collaboration graph is NOT an online social feed. The paper reports exponents scattered from 2.1 to 4 across its networks; the commonly repeated '2 to 3 for social networks' band does NOT appear in it. Later measurements of real platforms (Kwak 2010 for Twitter; Ugander 2011 for Facebook) explicitly reject a pure power-law for their degree distributions.
valid_uses: aggregate-fit diagnostic on the synthetic graph's tail exponent
degree_tail_exponent.verification_note: Verified 2026-07-13 by reading the arXiv full text. NOTE: the previous target value of 2.5 appears NOWHERE in this paper and was removed.

clustering.source: Mislove et al. 2007, 'Measurement and Analysis of Online Social Networks', IMC'07 Table 4 — mean/sd of the measured clustering coefficients of Flickr, LiveJournal, Orkut and YouTube
clustering.source_title: Measurement and Analysis of Online Social Networks
clustering.population: Flickr (1,846,198 users), LiveJournal (5,284,457), Orkut (3,072,441), YouTube (1,157,827) — public user-link crawls
clustering.time_window: Oct 2006 - Jan 2007 (per-network crawl dates in the paper's Table 1)
clustering.methodology: BFS crawl of public user links; DIRECTED clustering coefficient (links among a node's N neighbours divided by N(N-1))
clustering.statistic_location: Table 4 ('The observed clustering coefficient...'): Flickr 0.313, LiveJournal 0.330, Orkut 0.171, YouTube 0.136
clustering.transformation: mean (0.238) and sample standard deviation (0.098) computed across the four reported networks; the arithmetic is reproducible from the quoted table
clustering.tolerance_rationale: derived_from_source: sample standard deviation of the four measured networks (0.098). The paper separately reports a 0.040 sd across Orkut subsamples, so cross-network spread dominates.
clustering.applicability_limits: The paper measures a DIRECTED clustering coefficient; this simulator measures the standard UNDIRECTED coefficient, so the definitions are not interchangeable. The measured range (0.136-0.330) also straddles the frequently repeated '0.1-0.3' band rather than confirming it.
valid_uses: aggregate-fit diagnostic on the synthetic graph's clustering
clustering.verification_note: Verified 2026-07-13 from the paper's Table 4. The previous target of 0.2 is not any measured network's value.

diurnal_peak_hour.source: Golder, Wilkinson & Huberman 2007, 'Rhythms of Social Interaction: Messaging Within a Massive Online Network' — weekday messaging peak at 20-21h
diurnal_peak_hour.source_title: Rhythms of Social Interaction: Messaging Within a Massive Online Network
diurnal_peak_hour.population: 4.2 million Facebook users, 362 million messages and pokes — US COLLEGE STUDENTS (Facebook was college-only in this period)
diurnal_peak_hour.methodology: fully anonymized message headers aggregated by hour of week
diurnal_peak_hour.tolerance_rationale: derived_from_source: the paper names a 20-21h peak window, giving +/- 1 hour
diurnal_peak_hour.applicability_limits: US college students on 2005-2006 Facebook messaging; NOT a general-population or present-day posting rhythm, and messaging is not the same activity as public posting.
valid_uses: aggregate-fit diagnostic on the synthetic diurnal curve's peak
diurnal_peak_hour.verification_note: Verified 2026-07-13 from the paper's full text. The previous general-purpose 16-18h peak had no citable source and was removed.

diurnal_trough_hour.source: Golder, Wilkinson & Huberman 2007 — 'little messaging activity between roughly 3 and 8 am'
diurnal_trough_hour.source_title: Rhythms of Social Interaction: Messaging Within a Massive Online Network
diurnal_trough_hour.population: 4.2 million Facebook users (US college students), 362 million messages and pokes
diurnal_trough_hour.methodology: fully anonymized message headers aggregated by hour of week
diurnal_trough_hour.statistic_location: Sec.3.4: '...little messaging activity between roughly 3 and 8 am...'
diurnal_trough_hour.transformation: midpoint (5.5) of the paper's stated 3-8am low-activity window
diurnal_trough_hour.tolerance_rationale: derived_from_source: half-width (2.5h) of the paper's stated 3-8am window
diurnal_trough_hour.applicability_limits: Same population caveat as the peak: US college students, 2005-2006 Facebook messaging.
valid_uses: aggregate-fit diagnostic on the synthetic diurnal curve's trough
diurnal_trough_hour.verification_note: Verified 2026-07-13 from the paper's full text.

posts_per_agent_day.source: Pew Research Center 2019, 'Sizing Up Twitter Users' — MEAN posts/day derived from the published top-10%/bottom-90% decomposition
posts_per_agent_day.population: 2,791 US adult Twitter users (probability-based Ipsos KnowledgePanel recruitment, survey linked to actual account tweet data)
posts_per_agent_day.methodology: linked survey + observed account tweet volumes
posts_per_agent_day.statistic_location: 'the median user in the top 10% by tweet volume creates 138 tweets monthly, while the median user in the bottom 90% creates just two tweets per month'
posts_per_agent_day.transformation: DERIVED MEAN, not a published figure: (0.1*138 + 0.9*2)/30.4 ~= 0.51 posts/day. The published MEDIAN is 2 tweets/month ~= 0.066/day; this simulator's observable is a per-agent mean, so the mean is the comparable statistic.
posts_per_agent_day.tolerance_rationale: scenario_threshold_not_derived: Pew publishes no uncertainty on this decomposition and the distribution is extremely right-skewed; the tolerance is a scenario knob, not a confidence bound
posts_per_agent_day.applicability_limits: US adult Twitter users in 2018, derived (not published) as a mean over a heavily right-skewed distribution. Pew's 2021 follow-up is materially lower (bottom 75% of users: median zero tweets/month), so this figure is neither platform- nor period-stable.
valid_uses: aggregate-fit diagnostic on synthetic posting volume
invalid_uses: any claim about a MEDIAN or typical user
posts_per_agent_day.verification_note: Verified 2026-07-13. The prior justification ('median platform posting rates') was WRONG even where the number survives: 0.5 is defensible only as a derived mean, never as a median.

ad_ctr.source: Zhang, Yuan, Wang & Shen 2014, 'Real-Time Bidding Benchmarking with iPinYou Dataset' (arXiv:1407.7073) — measured display-advertising CTR
ad_ctr.source_title: Real-Time Bidding Benchmarking with iPinYou Dataset
ad_ctr.population: 9 advertiser campaigns on the iPinYou DSP (real-time-bidding display advertising logs; bids, impressions, clicks, conversions)
ad_ctr.methodology: per-campaign click/impression ratios computed from the released RTB logs
ad_ctr.statistic_location: Sec.2.3: 'all the advertisers has CTR less than 0.1% except for advertiser 2997 (0.444%)... 0.1% is usually around the average CTR for desktop display advertising in practice'
ad_ctr.transformation: the paper's stated typical desktop-display average (0.1% = 0.001) used as the target
ad_ctr.tolerance_rationale: derived_from_source: 8 of 9 campaigns fall below 0.001 with a single outlier at 0.00444; a +/-0.001 band covers the bulk of the observed campaign spread
ad_ctr.applicability_limits: DESKTOP DISPLAY real-time-bidding in China (2013) — NOT social-feed advertising, which is this simulator's ad surface. No named, publicly verifiable primary source for a social-feed CTR was found; the widely quoted ~1% figure comes only from commercial aggregators with proprietary, non-auditable samples, so it is not used here.
valid_uses: aggregate-fit diagnostic on synthetic ad click-through
invalid_uses: any claim about social-feed ad performance
ad_ctr.verification_note: Verified 2026-07-13. The previous 0.01 (1%) target was an order of magnitude above the only citable measurement and cited no named source.

appeal_grant_rate.source: YouTube / Google, California AB 587 Transparency Report H1 2024 — 82,190 reinstatements from 745,707 appeals of removed videos
appeal_grant_rate.source_title: YouTube California Assembly Bill 587 Transparency Report, H1 2024
appeal_grant_rate.population: all YouTube videos removed for Community Guidelines violations and appealed in the reporting period (16,793,180 removals; 745,707 appeals; 82,190 reinstatements)
appeal_grant_rate.geography: global enforcement (filed with the California Attorney General)
appeal_grant_rate.methodology: platform-reported enforcement counts filed under a statutory transparency obligation
appeal_grant_rate.statistic_location: Sec.04 'Information on actioned content': appeals received 745,707; appealed videos reinstated 82,190
appeal_grant_rate.transformation: ratio computed from the two published counts: 82,190 / 745,707 = 0.110
appeal_grant_rate.tolerance_rationale: derived_from_source: the per-policy reinstatement rates published in the same report span 6.0% (violent extremism) to 14.8% (hateful/abusive); +/-0.044 covers that spread around the 11.0% overall rate
appeal_grant_rate.applicability_limits: One platform (YouTube), one half-year, video removals only. Meta and TikTok omit the numeric tables from their AB 587 filings (citing the X Corp. v. Bonta injunction), so no cross-platform rate is derivable. The rate is not period-stable: reporting around Q4 2019 implies roughly double this figure.
valid_uses: aggregate-fit diagnostic on the synthetic appeals workflow
invalid_uses: compliance assessment of any real platform
appeal_grant_rate.verification_note: Verified 2026-07-13 from the filing's own counts. The previous 0.2-0.25 target was roughly double the only citable measurement and cited no named source.
